title: An Insurance Rate Hike In Frankfurt
gist: Paywall teaser: the ECB is expected to raise its deposit rate from 2% to 2.25% Thursday after a year on hold, kicking off a round of DM central bank meetings (BoJ, Fed, BoE next week) as policymakers respond to an energy-driven negative supply shock pushing inflation up and growth down; the open question is whether it's a one-off insurance hike or the start of a tightening cycle. Light card — intro only.

insights.claim: The ECB is expected to raise its deposit rate from 2% to 2.25% on Thursday after a year on hold.
insights.speaker: Cedric Gemehl, August Gudmundsson
insights.so_what: A near-consensus hike; the trade is in the forward guidance, not the decision itself.
insights.quote: There is little suspense around the ECB’s expected decision to raise its deposit rate from 2% to 2.25% after a year on hold.

insights.claim: The key uncertainty is whether the ECB hike is merely an insurance move against an adverse inflation shock or the start of a broader tightening cycle, to be judged from fresh projections and Lagarde's press conference.
insights.speaker: Cedric Gemehl, August Gudmundsson
insights.so_what: The one-off-vs-cycle read drives EUR rates and bund pricing — watch the projections and Lagarde's tone.
insights.quote: whether this is merely an insurance rate hike in response to an adverse inflation shock, or the start of a broader tightening cycle

insights.claim: A negative energy supply shock is simultaneously pushing inflation up and economic growth down, framing the policy dilemma for the ECB, BoJ, Fed and BoE meeting over the next week.
insights.speaker: Cedric Gemehl, August Gudmundsson
insights.so_what: Stagflationary supply shock means DM central banks face conflicting mandates — a cluster of meetings next week is a concentrated volatility window.
insights.quote: a negative supply shock in energy markets which is pushing inflation up and economic growth down
